text: 5. For item (a), if every prime divisor of 2b2 + 3 was congruent to ±1 modulo 8, we would have 2b2 + 3 = ±1 (mod 8); show that this is impossible. For (b), if (2b2 + 3) | (a2 − 2) and p is as in (a), then a2 = 2 (mod p), so that 2 would be a quadratic residue modulo p; use the result of Example 12.27 to reach a contradiction.

text: 6. Let Ln = 2x2. If n is odd, use item (b) of Problem 11, page 252, together with the fact that Ln is even, to show that n = 3 or 9 (mod 12). Then, apply several times item (e) of this same problem to get Ln = L3 or L9 = 4 (mod 8), and show that this gives x2 = 2 (mod 4), which is impossible. Now, assuming that n is even, consider the following three separate cases: n = O (mod 4), n = 6 (mod 8) or n = 2 (mod 8). If n = 0 (mod 4), write n = 2.3 .k, with k ∈ Z such that 2 | k, 3 † k. Argue as in the proof of Example 12.25 to obtain Ln = −L0 = −2 (mod Lk) and, hence, x2 = −1 (mod Lk). Show that this furnishes a contradiction, exactly as the one obtained in the proof of Example 12.25. If n = 6 (mod 8) but n = 6 (note that L6 = 18 = 2 . 32), write n = 6 + 2 . 3l . k, with k ∈ Z such that 4 | k and 3 † k. Use item (d) of Problem 13, page 253, to obtain Ln = −L6 = −18 (mod Lk), and hence x2 = −9 (mod Lk). Also as in the proof of Example 12.25, conclude that one can take a prime divisor p of Lk such that p = 3 (mod 4), so that x2 = −9 (mod p). Then, show that 3 † Lk, so that gcd(p, 9) = 1 and, thus, ( ) = (p )(− ) = ( ) = −1. Finally, in the case n ≡ 2 (mod 8), just note that L−n = Ln and −n ≡ 6 (mod 8).

text: 7. Let Fn = x2. If n = 1 (mod 4) but n ≠ 1, write n = 1 + 2 . 3l . k, with k ∈ Z such that 2 | k, 3 † k. Use item (c) of Problem 13, page 253, to get Fn = −F1 = −1 (mod Lk); then, arrive at a contradiction, exactly as in the proof of Example 12.25. If n = 3 (mod 4), use the fact that F−n = Fn and —n = 1 (mod 4). If n is even, write n = 2m and apply item (a) of Problem 13, page 253 to get Fn = Fm Lm. Then, distinguish two distinct subcases: if gcd(n, 3) = 1, use Problem 12, page 252 to conclude that Lm is also a perfect square; then, apply the result of Example 12.25. If gcd(n, 3) = 3, use Problem 12, page 252, again to conclude that Lm is twice a perfect square; then, apply the result of the previous problem.
